seoTitle: Delete Duplicate Transactions in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: How to find and permanently remove duplicate bank feed transactions in QuickBooks Online, whether already categorized or still in review.
intro: Duplicate expenses or twin deposits throw off your reconciliation fast, and QuickBooks Online handles cleanup a little differently depending on whether the duplicate is already recorded or still sitting in your review queue. This guide covers both scenarios.

steps.heading: Open your bank transactions
steps.body: Go to Transactions, then Bank Transactions, and select the account with the duplicate entries.

steps.heading: Undo already-categorized duplicates
steps.body: In the Categorized tab, check the boxes next to duplicate rows and click Undo to roll them back into the For Review queue.

steps.heading: Exclude duplicates from the review queue
steps.body: In the For Review tab, check the duplicate rows and click the Exclude command from the top action bar.

steps.heading: Go to the Excluded tab
steps.body: Navigate to the Excluded tab to see all the transactions you've flagged for removal.

steps.heading: Delete permanently
steps.body: Check the master checkbox at the top of the column, then click Delete to wipe the duplicates so they never affect reconciliation again.

faq.question: What's the difference between excluding and deleting a transaction?
faq.answer: Excluding removes it from your active balance calculations while keeping a record, but deleting from the Excluded tab wipes it permanently.

faq.question: How do I fix a duplicate that's already been categorized?
faq.answer: Use Undo in the Categorized tab first to send it back to For Review, then exclude and delete it from there.

faq.question: Will excluding a transaction affect my account balance?
faq.answer: Excluded transactions don't count toward your live balance, which is exactly why they need to move there before deletion.

faq.question: How do I stop duplicates from happening again?
faq.answer: Review your active automation rule parameters, since overly broad matching rules are a common cause of systematic duplicate entries.
